text: When setting project goals, using the SMART framework helps ensure clarity. A SMART goal is Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound. For example, instead of saying "improve the website," you could say "increase website traffic by 20% within three months." To track progress, break the goal into milestones—key checkpoints like completing user research or launching a new feature. Each milestone leads to specific deliverables, which are tangible outcomes such as a report or a prototype. Finally, stakeholder alignment is crucial. This means discussing and agreeing on goals with everyone involved, from team members to clients. When everyone understands the vision, the project runs more smoothly and avoids misunderstandings.
